old_claim.note: Mechanical Clock Workday Regulation provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: Railway scheduling and regional clock coordination drove standard time; medieval civic workday regulation was not a direct prerequisite.

source_shape.source_locator: Standard Time section: explains the railroad scheduling problem, William F. Allen's five-zone plan, and adoption of Standard Railway Time on November 18, 1883.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: North American railroads and communities adopted Standard Railway Time on November 18, 1883.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The scoped evidence establishes the milestone without the removed dependency. Railway scheduling and regional clock coordination drove standard time; medieval civic workday regulation was not a direct prerequisite.

ontology_before: standard_time_zones treated mechanical_clock_workday_regulation as a direct causal prerequisite.
ontology_after: standard_time_zones is scoped to the directly evidenced mechanism and no longer claims mechanical_clock_workday_regulation as a prerequisite.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed and locked by graph invariant 2026-07-12-structural-accuracy-batch: standard_time_zones no longer directly depends on mechanical_clock_workday_regulation.
would_reject_if: A reliable source demonstrates that every implementation within the current standard_time_zones scope technically requires mechanical_clock_workday_regulation.
short_reason: Railway scheduling and regional clock coordination drove standard time; medieval civic workday regulation was not a direct prerequisite.
